cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about understanding what the headline "Safina goes from basket case to top of the world" implies. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to understand the context in which the headline was mentioned. The article talks about Dinara Safina, a tennis player who was previously overshadowed by her brother Marat Safin but has now become the world No. 1. The headline is from The Sydney Morning Herald during the Australian Open. The phrase "basket case" is an idiom. I think "basket case" usually refers to someone who is very nervous, emotional, or unstable—maybe even someone who can't control their emotions. So, the headline is saying that Safina was once a basket case (emotionally unstable or struggling) and now she's at the top of her game. Looking at the options: A) She wanted to top the world when she was a baby in a basket. That seems incorrect because "basket case" isn't about being in a baby basket. It's more about emotional state. B) After winning the Australian Open, she cried. The headline doesn't mention crying specifically. It's more about her emotional transformation. C) Too nervous at first, but finally gained confidence and won. This could be possible. If "basket case" refers to being nervous, then gaining confidence and reaching the top makes sense. D) She learned from the basket case and became top. The phrase "basket case" doesn't mean someone to learn from. It's more about her own emotional state.
